docs.title: integMET Documentation
docs.text: Welcome to the documentation for integMET, an integrated metabolomics database.

docs.title: What is integMET?
docs.text:
integMET is a database that integrates metabolomics data from public repositories and makes them comparable across studies.

The key innovation is the Differential Profile (DiffProf) — a standardized representation of metabolite changes between two biological conditions. By converting diverse metabolomics studies into DiffProfs, integMET enables cross-study discovery and reuse.

docs.title: Get Started
docs.text: → Quick Start Guide — Learn how to use integMET in 5 minutes

docs.title: Learn More
docs.text: Section Description Quick Start How to navigate and use integMET Concepts Core ideas behind Differential Profiles Data Model How Studies, DiffProfs, and Metabolites relate Tutorial Network visualization guide

docs.title: Database Statistics
docs.text:
• 28,493 Metabolites
  • 3,871 Studies
  • 1,204 Pathways
  • 12 Species

docs.title: The Challenge: Isolated Metabolomics Studies
docs.text:
Metabolomics studies generate rich data about small molecules in biological samples. However, most studies exist as isolated datasets:

  • Each study uses different experimental designs
  • Sample groups and conditions vary across studies
  • Raw data formats and processing methods differ
  • Results are difficult to compare directly

This limits the potential for reuse and discovery across studies.

docs.title: The Solution: Differential Profiles
docs.text: integMET addresses this challenge by introducing the Differential Profile (DiffProf) as the fundamental unit of data.

docs.title: What is a Differential Profile?
docs.text:
A Differential Profile captures how metabolites change between two biological conditions.

For example: - Disease vs. Control - Before treatment vs. After treatment - Mutant vs. Wild type - Stressed vs. Normal

Each DiffProf contains: - Two comparison groups (Group A vs. Group B) - Metabolite fold-changes between the groups - Statistical significance (p-values) for each change - Direction of change (up or down)

docs.title: From Raw Data to Comparable Profiles
docs.text:
Traditional metabolomics produces a data matrix: rows of metabolites, columns of samples, cells containing abundance values.

integMET converts this into DiffProfs:

Traditional Data Matrix       →      Differential Profile

  Sample1  Sample2  ...              Group A vs Group B
  Met1: 100    150                   Met1: ratio=1.5, ↑
  Met2: 200     80                   Met2: ratio=0.4, ↓
  ...                                ...

By standardizing data into DiffProfs, integMET makes different studies comparable.

docs.title: 1. Universal Comparison Unit
docs.text: Every biological comparison — regardless of species, tissue, or experimental platform — becomes a DiffProf. This creates a common language for metabolomics data.

docs.title: 2. Focus on Biological Contrast
docs.text: A DiffProf represents a biological event or contrast, not just raw measurements. This captures the biologically meaningful signal: what changes when conditions differ.

docs.title: 3. Cross-Study Discovery
docs.text:
With thousands of DiffProfs in a standardized format, you can:

  • Find studies with similar metabolic signatures
  • Discover unexpected connections between different biological phenomena
  • Identify metabolites that consistently change across related conditions

docs.title: Key Terminology
docs.text: Term Definition Study A metabolomics experiment from a public repository (e.g., MetaboLights) Differential Profile (DiffProf) A standardized record of metabolite changes between two conditions Metabolite A small molecule tracked across studies and DiffProfs iDMET A similarity metric that quantifies how similar two DiffProfs are based on shared metabolite changes

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
• Learn about the Data Model to understand how Studies, DiffProfs, and Metabolites relate
  • Explore the Tutorial to see how DiffProf similarity networks work

docs.title: Quick Start
docs.text: This guide helps you get started with integMET in a few minutes.

docs.title: What You Can Do
docs.text:
• Browse Studies — Find metabolomics studies by condition, disease, or species
  • Explore Differential Profiles — See how metabolites change between biological conditions
  • Find Metabolites — Search for specific metabolites and trace their changes across studies

docs.title: Navigation Overview
docs.text:
Home
├── Studies → click a study → see its DiffProfs
├── Diff Profiles → click a DiffProf → see metabolite changes
└── Metabolites → click a metabolite → see where it changes

docs.title: Workflow A: Start from a Condition
docs.text:
"I'm interested in a disease or condition — what metabolites change?"

  1. Go to Studies
  2. Browse or search for studies related to your condition
  3. Click a study to see its details
  4. View the Differential Profiles from that study
  5. Examine the Top Hits — metabolites with significant changes

docs.title: Workflow B: Start from a Metabolite
docs.text:
"I have a metabolite of interest — where does it change?"

  1. Go to Metabolites
  2. Find your metabolite by name or identifier
  3. Click to view its detail page
  4. See Studies Observed — where this metabolite was detected
  5. See DiffProfs with Variation — where this metabolite changed significantly

docs.title: Workflow C: Find Similar Phenomena
docs.text:
"I found an interesting DiffProf — are there similar ones?"

  1. Go to Diff Profiles
  2. Find a DiffProf of interest
  3. Note the metabolites that change
  4. Use the network visualization to find DiffProfs with similar metabolic signatures

docs.title: Reading a DiffProf
docs.text:
When you view a Differential Profile, you'll see:

Field Meaning Group A vs Group B The two conditions being compared Significant Features Number of metabolites with significant changes Top Hits List of changed metabolites Ratio Fold-change (>1 = increased, <1 = decreased in Group B) Direction up or down in Group B relative to Group A

docs.title: Reading a Metabolite Page
docs.text:
When you view a Metabolite, you'll see:

Field Meaning InChIKey Unique chemical identifier Studies Observed All studies where this metabolite was detected DiffProfs with Variation DiffProfs where this metabolite changed significantly

docs.title: Tips
docs.text:
• Start broad, then narrow down — Browse studies first, then drill into specific DiffProfs
  • Use metabolites as links — A metabolite connects multiple studies and conditions
  • Look for patterns — If a metabolite changes across multiple DiffProfs, it may indicate a common biological response

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
• Read Concepts to understand Differential Profiles in depth
  • Explore the Data Model to understand how data is organized
  • Learn about Network Visualization to find similar DiffProfs

TODO: Add screenshots of the integMET interface

docs.title: Differential Profile (DiffProf)
docs.text: A Differential Profile (DiffProf) is the core data unit in integMET. It represents a biological contrast between two sample groups, together with the metabolite changes observed.

docs.title: What is a DiffProf?
docs.text:
A DiffProf captures a specific biological comparison:

  • Group A (reference) vs. Group B (condition of interest)
  • Which metabolites changed significantly
  • The direction and magnitude of each change

Each DiffProf represents a biological event or contrast — such as disease onset, treatment effect, or developmental change — expressed through metabolite changes.

docs.title: Basic Information
docs.text: Field Description DiffProf ID Unique identifier (e.g., MTBLS10_0001_00000005) Study ID Parent study this DiffProf belongs to Title Comparison name (e.g., Control_vs_HIAE) Group A Reference group name and description Group B Condition group name and description

docs.title: Statistical Parameters
docs.text: Field Description Total Features Number of metabolites tested Significant Features Number of metabolites showing significant change p-value Threshold Statistical significance cutoff (e.g., 0.05) Ratio Threshold Fold-change cutoff for significance (e.g., >1.5 or <1/1.5)

docs.title: Significant Metabolites (Top Hits)
docs.text:
Each significant metabolite includes:

Field Description Metabolite ID InChIKey identifier Ratio Fold-change between Group B and Group A p-value Statistical significance Direction up (increased in Group B) or down (decreased in Group B)

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
DiffProf: MTBLS103_0001_00000001
Title: Control_vs_HIAE

Group A: Control group with no HIAE condition
Group B: Group with HIAE condition

Total Features: 9
Significant Features: 6
p-value Threshold: 0.05

Top Hits:
  - methionine sulfoxide: ratio=0.37, p=3.7e-06, down
  - glutamylglycine: ratio=0.51, p=1.2e-06, down
  - Valyl-Glutamate: ratio=0.45, p=4.2e-05, down
  - L-glutamate: ratio=0.62, p=3.7e-05, down

docs.title: Understanding Direction
docs.text:
• up: Metabolite level is higher in Group B than Group A
  • down: Metabolite level is lower in Group B than Group A

The ratio indicates the magnitude: - ratio = 2.0 means Group B has 2× the level of Group A - ratio = 0.5 means Group B has half the level of Group A

docs.title: Relationship to Studies and Metabolites
docs.text:
Study (MTBLS103)
└── DiffProf (Control_vs_HIAE)
    ├── Metabolite: methionine sulfoxide (down)
    ├── Metabolite: glutamylglycine (down)
    └── ...
  • Each DiffProf belongs to exactly one Study
  • Each DiffProf contains multiple Metabolites with their fold-changes
  • The same Metabolite can appear in many DiffProfs across different Studies

docs.title: Metabolite
docs.text: A Metabolite is a small molecule tracked across the integMET database. Each metabolite records where it was observed and where it showed significant changes.

docs.title: What is a Metabolite in integMET?
docs.text:
integMET tracks metabolites across all Studies and DiffProfs. For each metabolite, you can find:

  • Which Studies detected this metabolite
  • Which DiffProfs show significant changes in this metabolite
  • Chemical identifiers for external database lookup

This allows you to trace a metabolite back to its biological contexts — discovering which conditions, treatments, or diseases affect its levels.

docs.title: Chemical Identifiers
docs.text: Field Description InChIKey Unique structural identifier (e.g., SNICXCGAKADSCV-UHFFFAOYSA-N) ChEBI ID ChEBI database identifier IUPAC Name Systematic chemical name Synonym Common name (e.g., "Rutin", "Reserpin") Reactome IDs Links to Reactome pathways

docs.title: Observation Tracking
docs.text: Field Description Studies Observed List of Studies where this metabolite was detected DiffProfs Observed List of DiffProfs where this metabolite appears Studies with Variation Studies where this metabolite showed significant change DiffProfs with Variation DiffProfs where this metabolite showed significant change

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
Metabolite: Reserpin
InChIKey: QEVHRUUCFGRFIF-MDEJGZGSSA-N
ChEBI ID: 28487

Observed in 9 studies:
  MTBLS10, MTBLS3750, MTBLS3963, MTBLS4463, ...

Significant variation in 3 DiffProfs:
  MTBLS3750_0002_00000006
  MTBLS4463_0001_00000001
  MTBLS5195_0001_00000001

docs.title: Tracing Metabolites to Biological Contexts
docs.text:
A key feature of integMET is the ability to ask: "Where does this metabolite change?"

Starting from a metabolite, you can:

  1. Find all DiffProfs where it shows significant variation
  2. Examine the biological contrasts in those DiffProfs
  3. Discover patterns across different conditions
Metabolite: Rutin
└── Significant in DiffProfs:
    ├── MTBLS1191: Disease vs Control (liver condition)
    ├── MTBLS2384: Treated vs Untreated (drug response)
    └── MTBLS3518: Stressed vs Normal (environmental stress)

This reveals that Rutin levels change across diverse biological contexts — potentially indicating shared metabolic responses.

docs.title: Relationship to Studies and DiffProfs
docs.text:
Metabolite (Rutin)
├── Observed in Study: MTBLS1191
│   └── Significant in DiffProf: MTBLS1191_0001_00000001
├── Observed in Study: MTBLS2384
│   └── Significant in DiffProf: MTBLS2384_0002_00000003
└── ...
  • One Metabolite can appear in many Studies
  • One Metabolite can show significant changes in many DiffProfs
  • This cross-referencing enables discovery of shared metabolic patterns

docs.title: Data Model Overview
docs.text: integMET organizes data into three core entities: Study, Differential Profile (DiffProf), and Metabolite.

docs.title: Entity Relationships
docs.text:
Study (e.g., MTBLS10)
└── DiffProf (e.g., Control_0h vs Wounding_72h)
    └── Metabolite (e.g., nicotine — ratio=0.62, down)
  • One Study contains one or more DiffProfs (biological contrasts)
  • One DiffProf captures a biological event with its metabolite changes
  • One Metabolite can appear across multiple DiffProfs and Studies

docs.title: Relationships Summary
docs.text: From To Relationship Study DiffProf One Study contains one or more DiffProfs DiffProf Study Each DiffProf belongs to exactly one Study DiffProf Metabolite One DiffProf contains many Metabolites (with fold-changes) Metabolite DiffProf One Metabolite can appear in many DiffProfs Metabolite Study One Metabolite can appear in many Studies

docs.title: Study
docs.text:
A Study represents a metabolomics experiment from a public repository such as MetaboLights.

  • Identifier: MetaboLights accession (e.g., MTBLS10)
  • Contains: Title, summary, observations, findings
  • Linked metadata: Disease terms, gene names, species, chemicals

→ Learn more about Study

docs.title: Differential Profile (DiffProf)
docs.text:
A DiffProf represents a biological contrast or event — a comparison between two sample groups that captures a specific biological phenomenon.

Examples of biological contrasts: - Disease vs. Healthy - Treated vs. Untreated - Regenerating tissue vs. Intact tissue - Stressed vs. Normal

Each DiffProf carries biological meaning: it describes what changed at the metabolite level when a specific condition or treatment was applied.

  • Identifier: Study ID + comparison index (e.g., MTBLS10_0001_00000005)
  • Contains: Two comparison groups, metabolite fold-changes, p-values, direction of change
  • Biological meaning: Each DiffProf represents a distinct biological event with its metabolic signature

→ Learn more about DiffProf

docs.title: Metabolite
docs.text:
A Metabolite is a small molecule tracked across the database.

  • Identifier: InChIKey (e.g., SNICXCGAKADSCV-UHFFFAOYSA-N)
  • Contains: Chemical identifiers (ChEBI, IUPAC name, synonyms)
  • Tracks: Which Studies and DiffProfs this metabolite appears in

→ Learn more about Metabolite

docs.title: Navigation Example
docs.text:
A typical exploration path:

  1. Start with a Study — browse studies by keyword or condition
  2. View its DiffProfs — see what biological comparisons were made
  3. Examine Metabolites — find which metabolites changed significantly
  4. Trace Metabolite across DiffProfs — discover other conditions where the same metabolite changes

docs.title: Study
docs.text: A Study represents a metabolomics experiment from a public data repository or published research.

docs.title: What is a Study?
docs.text:
Each Study in integMET corresponds to a research project that generated metabolomics data. Studies are typically derived from:

  • Entries in metabolomics data repositories (e.g., MetaboLights)
  • Open access publications with available data

From each Study, integMET extracts sample information and data matrices to create Differential Profiles (DiffProfs).

docs.title: Metadata Annotation
docs.text:
integMET independently annotates each Study with structured metadata using natural language processing (NLP) tools. This allows users to search and filter studies by:

  • Disease terms
  • Gene names
  • Chemical compounds
  • Species
  • Gene Ontology terms

docs.title: Study Fields
docs.text: Field Description Study ID Unique identifier (e.g., MTBLS10) Source Link to original repository entry Title Original study title Summary Brief description of the study Observation What was measured or observed Findings Key results from the study

docs.title: Annotated Metadata
docs.text: Field Description Example Disease MeSH disease terms hyperinsulinaemic androgen excess, metabolic syndrome Gene Gene names apo-A1, apolipoprotein-A1 Chemical Chemical compounds mentioned nitrogen Species NCBI Taxonomy species Homo sapiens, Nicotiana attenuata GO Terms Gene Ontology annotations —

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
Study: MTBLS103
Title: Metabolomics reveals impaired maturation of HDL particles
       in adolescents with hyperinsulinaemic androgen excess
Summary: HDL maturation impaired in HIAE adolescents.
Observation: Serum levels of methionine sulfoxide in HIAE girls
Findings: Impaired HDL maturation linked to methionine-148 oxidation in apo-A1

Annotated diseases: hyperinsulinaemic androgen excess, metabolic syndrome,
                    hypertriglyceridemia, type 2 diabetes
Annotated genes: apo-A1

docs.title: Relationship to DiffProfs
docs.text:
One Study can contain multiple DiffProfs. Each DiffProf represents a specific biological comparison made within the Study.

For example, a study comparing disease vs. control across multiple time points might generate several DiffProfs:

Study: MTBLS103
├── DiffProf: Control vs HIAE (comparison 1)
├── DiffProf: Control vs HIAE (comparison 2)
└── ...

→ Learn more about Differential Profiles

docs.title: Network Visualization
docs.text: integMET provides a network visualization that reveals similarities between Differential Profiles based on their metabolite changes.

docs.title: Concept
docs.text:
When two biological conditions cause similar metabolite changes, their DiffProfs are likely related — even if they come from different studies, species, or experimental contexts.

integMET quantifies this similarity using iDMET (integrated Differential Metabolite) scores and visualizes the relationships as a network.

docs.title: Nodes
docs.text: Each node represents a DiffProf — a biological contrast with its associated metabolite changes.

docs.title: Edges
docs.text:
An edge connects two DiffProfs when they share similar metabolic changes. The similarity is calculated based on:

  • Overlapping metabolites that change significantly in both DiffProfs
  • Similar direction of change (both up or both down)
  • Magnitude of the changes

docs.title: iDMET Score
docs.text: The iDMET score quantifies how similar two DiffProfs are based on their shared metabolite patterns. Higher scores indicate stronger metabolic similarity.

docs.title: Exploring the Network
docs.text:
The network visualization allows you to:

  1. Find related phenomena — Discover DiffProfs with similar metabolic signatures
  2. Identify clusters — Groups of connected DiffProfs may represent related biological processes
  3. Generate hypotheses — Unexpected connections between conditions may suggest shared mechanisms

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
Network cluster example:

    [Diabetes vs Control]
           |
           | (high iDMET)
           |
    [Obesity vs Lean] -------- [Metabolic Syndrome vs Healthy]
                      (high iDMET)

In this example, three DiffProfs from different studies cluster together because they share similar metabolite changes — suggesting related metabolic disruptions across these conditions.

docs.title: Finding Similar Conditions
docs.text:
Starting from a DiffProf of interest, find other conditions with similar metabolic profiles:

  1. Select your DiffProf in the network
  2. Examine connected nodes (high iDMET edges)
  3. Explore the biological contexts of similar DiffProfs

docs.title: Cross-Species Discovery
docs.text:
The network can reveal conserved metabolic responses across species:

  • A stress response in plants may connect to a similar response in animals
  • Drug effects in model organisms may link to human disease states

docs.title: Meta-Analysis
docs.text: Use the network to identify groups of related DiffProfs for combined analysis, increasing statistical power and generalizability.

docs.title: Limitations
docs.text:
• Similarity is based on detected metabolites; undetected metabolites are not compared
  • Different analytical platforms may detect different metabolite subsets
  • High iDMET scores suggest similarity but do not prove mechanistic relationships
